sentences.text: Jane Lester.
J-A-N-E L-E-S-T-E-R.
December 13, 1914 I was born.
Well, add it up. 81.
Kane, Pennsylvania.
They called me Jane of Kane.
It's a little town in Pennsylvania.
And my great-grandfather sent a card to Jane of Kane, and it was delivered-- because my family was noted.
They had the movie theater in the town, which was a very rare thing in 1914.
No recollection.
I left there in early childhood.
I went back to the home of my parents.
We all came from Brockport, New York, suburb of, oh, 20 miles from Rochester.
Eastman Kodak, Rochester, New York, you know?
Well, my family are, as with many Americans, derived from many European immigrants.
One line comes from Britain, Great Britain, from Germany, from Ireland-- and many-- in the way that many American families are-- a little bit of French.
Who knows?
Perhaps American Indian.
Who knows?
[LAUGHS]
Well, primarily, he was a merchant, clothing-- clothing and dry goods.
No.
No, she was most frustrated and stayed home, and wished she had not been so-- what should I say?
Reluctant to keep on with school work, and stayed home and had a large family.
Typical spinster, but she had a large family.
[LAUGHS]
Well, I was the oldest of five children.
And we grew up in western New York.
I think I had 65 living, rather close relatives when I was a little girl.
And we're always-- my mother couldn't get as far away as she wanted to from the place she came from, and was about 13 miles away on Elm Grove Road, upstate New York suburb.
They were three-- they came three-- after me, I had two brothers and a little sister.
And 15, 16 years after I was born, came our little-- our youngest, Elizabeth Ann.
My father was a Catholic, and my mother was a-- a Protestant.
And Catholics were regarded as-- in this little Protestant town-- as rather-- something not quite understood, and not wanted, particularly.
It was really a-- [LAUGHS] a mixed marriage, you can say.
My father-- at that time, the Catholic church was very conservative-- had to swear he would bring any ensuing children from the marriage up as Catholics.
And my mother always planned to keep the little one, at least, to go to church with her.
But she was rather a little tired with all her family work, and never managed to get up on Sunday and take her off.
[LAUGHS] So yes, I'd say my father was a good Catholic.
Oh, through our school, of course.
In those days, it was not illegal to read from the Old Testament.
We knew the Old Testament in our public school almost by heart.
And today, I guess it's illegal.

But at any rate, I don't know whether you would call it religious, but the fascinating stories of the Old Testament are well known to all who went to school in the simple schools and public schools in those days.
And then, my mother wanted me to be a blonde.
I had dark hair.
And she saw a beautiful blonde neighbor, a French girl-- French by derivation-- Blanche.
Blanche St.
Pierre was a beautiful blonde girl of 18-- and she wanted me to become beautiful like that when I was 18, and sent me to a strict Catholic school, Nazareth Academy.
I didn't turn blonde, of course.
And I went eight years to these Catholic schools, very-- I would say classical, high-level education.
They turned me into a Latin teacher, unemployed Latin teacher.
[LAUGHS] And I was-- went to school to the nuns in Rochester, New York.
They gave us really a wonderful training, and many of the students in the school were Catholic, Protestant, Jewish, whatever, you know?
But it was-- the nuns had charge of the training.
Now, there are hardly any nuns left.
So it has become almost publicly owned-- the school, Nazareth College in Rochester.
But it has a very high reputation.
Well, at that time, before World War II, there weren't so very many Negroes in the upper part of America.
And like anything strange, people didn't know well any Negroes.
We had a couple of Negroes in our school, in the public schools of Rochester-- there was good training together in the-- in the school system.

But I would say Eisenhower, World War I-- World War II, and the immediate post-war have made many changes in any feeling of color, racial antagonisms, and so forth-- have changed things very much from those days.
To tell you the truth, I wouldn't know which ones were Jewish and which weren't.
It was not much of a point with any of us, you know?
But there were quite a few interesting Jewish people in Rochester, New York, where we were centered-- Rochester, New York.
And it was because of some of these people-- I personally didn't happen to know them-- that I went to Europe, and my life changed quite a bit.
They had relatives in Germany who wanted to prepare themselves, should Hitler be going to stay, to get out of it and come to their relatives in America.

And so they told a classmate of mine, Ingemouse-- Ingeborg Giese-- who had been born-- born in Germany and come to America as a 12-year-old-- they knew she was going back as an unemployed schoolteacher, just as myself.
She was going back to her native Germany, to Hamburg.
And they thought, would she please visit their relatives and give them some English instruction?
And she said, sure, she would, and took the addresses and everything.
And went over and gradually brought over, I would say, five or six of us classmates, unemployed teachers-- I mean, we had little jobs.
We could make $13 a week in a department store or something, but we wanted-- we had all this training and wanted to get teaching jobs.
There just was no opportunity.
Especially for Catholic school graduates at that moment in that area.
But at any rate, she brought us one after the other over to Hamburg, Germany.

And we spent a period of time there, several months or so, had teaching experience on our records in a Berlitz, in another language school, teaching English-- to the many people in Germany who wanted to learn English, because they wanted to get out, and because Germans could not travel at that time.
Germany's a big travel company of today-- country of today.
Could take only-- unless they had a very good political reason-- could take only 10 marks with them out-- out of the country.
Now, you can't live in England, you can't study in Italy without a little money, you know.
So they all came to us as to a foreign country to learn English-- become an English correspondent in some business, or learn English just for the culture of it.
And we were busy, busy, busy.
We earned [GERMAN]-- that's about $0.30 an hour.
But we were getting teaching experience, and it was enough to live on.
And so we rather neglected these Jewish families down in other parts of Germany who were waiting for us to come and teach them and their children something of English.
But gradually, one after another, we went down and lived with them for a time, as I did.
I went down in '38-- this is Hitler Germany.
And I, you must remember, was really quite ignorant of all this politics.
I could speak only a handful of words of German.
I could barely read a few headlines in the newspaper.
And we were protected.
We were a foreign school.
The top of the school, Herr Director Sander in Hamburg, had his breast covered with medals of German National Socialist Party, more or less a cover for his very fine business there.
But he had all these strange foreigners there-- British people, Australians, French, making money, making their little bit of money, teaching the language that they had.
When-- we went down, then, when business got poor.

And business got very poor at a rather horrifying time, you probably have heard about, called Crystal Night-- when they went through the towns, these Nazis, because a German-- a German diplomat, a minor German diplomat, had been murdered in the German embassy in Paris.
They took vengeance, allegedly-- just wild destructiveness, went through the town, broke all the businesses'-- the windows.
The streets were all full of broken windows, broken glass.
And our students were very largely arrested, because it was predominantly what Hitler would call Jewish students.
They may or may not have been baptized Lutherans or once in a while Catholics.
But for him, they were-- even if they baptized themselves back 150 years ago-- they were Jewish race, as he called it.
He called it race.
And should be-- we all know what he had in mind.
But it didn't penetrate very quickly that he meant Hitler business, you know?
I mean, there had been anti-SemitIsm in the world.
People knew.
There was anti-Irish in Boston, there was a anti-Italian wherever there are a large-- a lot of it in America-- immigrant Italians.
Nobody knew that Hitler meant what he was saying.

A few people believed him, and it registered right away what Hitler amounted to, and what he would do for the poor Jewish people, for for the Germans, too, for the Europeans, for the-- it changed my life-- for the Americans.
They didn't know what he would do.

But there is a man, Robert Kempner, who knew-- even as Hitler arrived, and before he arrived, in the 1920s, when he was rampaging around Germany, getting the riff-Raff of Germany together to follow him in the streets-- and Germany had many problems, it is true.
Economic problems, unemployment, and people with little to do following Hitler in the streets, as they say, like the pied piper of Hamelin and loving Hitler and swearing by Hitler.

And this man, Robert Kempner, born a few weeks before the end of the century, in October before the new century-- not our coming new century, but the 1900 beginning-- realized in very early life-- he was very active politically and mentally and already a journalist in his 20s and a lawyer, and went into government work.
And he realized, that man is poison for everyone here.
Yes.
I arrived in October, 1937.
I was under treatment by a medical doctor.
And he did not want me to go to Germany.
But he was, perhaps, politically alert.
He said, don't go to Germany.
But I kind of escaped from him as my bandages-- as I got better able to take care of myself.
I wanted to start making some money and to get into teaching or get into something beyond Sibley's basement, you know?
And so I went, October 1937.
And my friend Ingemouse-- her poppy called her Ingemouse-- little Ingeborg-- and her mother was there.
My mother said she wouldn't let me go if Mrs.
Giese wasn't there.
Mrs.
Giese had gone over to chaperone Ingeborg, who was 22, 23, as I was.
I wouldn't let you go if Mrs.
Giese wasn't there, she said.
And we went over to Mama Giese-- I went over to Mama Giese and to Ingeborg.
And they met me at the railway station in Hamburg.
And I was in Germany.
I lived with Mama Giese and her daughter, Ingeborg.
They left Papa Giese in America.
He would never put foot on German soil again, he said.
They might draft him into the army.
He didn't know what would happen.
But Mama and Inge were there.
And Inge was enrolled for a course in psychiatry at Hamburg University, and also teaching-- very good teacher, she was.
Had a very successful career later.

Was teaching in the Berlitz School, and a second school that Herr and Frau director had organized-- where students came from all over Germany in their teens and 20s, and studied-- they couldn't go to England-- but they could come to the Hamburg language schools.
Which were quite well known in Germany, I think about the best with national advertising.
Always had work, no problem.
Paid very little, but enough to live on.
At any rate, getting there was funny.
Mama Giese, of course, like Germans do-- I don't know whether you know about that-- in the afternoon, especially on Sunday, they have coffee and kuchen.
And I was sometimes trusted-- I went to church at that time.
I was rather an earnest person and wanted to pray for my relatives while I was so far away.
And on the way home from church, I went to the wonderful German cake store and picked out the cake for the afternoon coffee cake, and took it home to Mama Giese and Ingeborg.
And they invited some friends, and then we'd have a coffee on Sunday afternoon.
And on the way, I was quite puzzled, but it happened to me several times.
People would step up to me and say, do you know-- in Deutsche, but I could see what they wanted-- do you know where such and such a street is?
And I thought, do they think I-- do they think I know where these streets are?
You know?
And I thought, well, I suppose I look like a German, too.
You know?
I was-- [LAUGHS] I felt so strange, as an American girl, just dropped into this area.
And I was quite puzzled that they often would come up and-- not in a mean way-- would say, we can't find such a street, or something like that.
It occurred to me, gradually, that Germans didn't look any different from anyone else.
And [LAUGHS] anyway, one got educated as one moved around, you know?
But it was a little interesting experience, because I went through all this without any knowledge of German to speak of, hardly.
You know-- and many times, strange.
Hmm?
Well, Hitler came to town, and we had a day off.
That was a nice feature.
[LAUGHS] But we lived a quite isolated life, we teachers.
We might have been about 40 in number, and our work-- sometimes 11 hours a day.
I mean, we came in the morning, had our card written up with the people-- individual or class groups of three up to 15 or 20-- that we would spend the hours of the day with.
And these teachers were really our environment, to a large extent-- British.
One of them was executed as a useless eater, because she got a little-- later, of course, after I left.
She got a little strange as people-- or seemed to them strange.
She was very outspoken, of course.
And they kind of eliminated her as a useless eater, an expression developed by Hitler.
And we lived among ourselves, so to speak.
It was actually forbidden us to associate with the students, who all wanted a free hour by taking us out dancing, or to coffee or something.
There was a little illicit relationship.
You know-- we would go out sometimes with a student, or a nice student, or a group of students.
But not so many.
But Germany-- I saw their flags.
They had their flags hung out.
And they didn't do it the way normal people, Americans, would do.
They didn't have a flag out on the house-- they had a flag, and about 3/4 of a meter farther, they had flag on flag on flag.
And you'd see these endless swastikas, you know.
And they were always marching.
You'd see the groups marching, soldiers marching, all sorts of people marching.
They were busy.
They were preparing for war.
They may not have known it, but they were preparing for war.
And Dr.
Kempner realized this.
Yes.
I think you will be a little bit horrified and perhaps disappointed-- I don't know.
But I slept through the Holocaust.
I found a room right near the-- when Frau Giese went home to America with her daughter, Ingemouse-- because she got engaged to a Jewish man.
And Mama Giese thought that was horrible.
And I must say, I got a little room.
I was formerly living, seemed like, on the outskirts, in the home of-- rented-- rented apartment of Mama Giese and Ingeborg.
I got a little room with the Silverstein sisters, right next to the [GERMAN].
They, too, were preparing to go to America.
But for the moment, they had an extra room in their apartment and were glad to have me rent it.
And it was walking distance for me to [GERMAN], where our school was.
And were very nice for me.
But they left shortly afterward for America, thank goodness.
And what was-- Kristallnacht, I came to school in the morning, and we had nobody on our list.
They were all in concentration camps, our students.
And they said, well, don't you know what happened?
My sophisticated people who had been there, my associates who had been there a long time-- I was a relative newcomer.
Don't you know what happened?
They broke all the Jewish business-- broke into all the Jewish businesses and robbed them.
And go downtown and look, they said.
Go down and look at it.
The whole streets are-- you have to watch out you don't get cut.
Glass is all over all the streets.
And I didn't know it.
I slept peacefully through it.
Arrived, and was puzzled and puzzled.
Why no students?
But I learned shortly-- I did go downtown, of course.
And it was a mess.
And for weeks, we had no students.
So at that moment, I telephoned Herr Martine Sachs down in Silesia, down in Hirschberg-- a beautiful town, still beautiful.
Today it's a Polish area.
And I went down there to see Herr Sachs.
He was a relative of relatives of relatives in Rochester.
And he had said that his little daughter, he wanted her to learn English.
And he might have to go to America, too.
And would this lady from Hamburg come over to his home in Silesia?
So I telephoned him when we had no work to do and no pay, of course, in Hamburg, and said, how would it be if I come now?
And his family were there.
And they said, oh yes.
That would be fine.
What I didn't know was that he, too, had been arrested, that he was in a concentration camp.
So I packed up and went through Germany without much knowledge of German.
But the Germans behaved in a very orderly fashion, no terrorism on the streets like we have today in these bad days.
Hitler ran a good shop.
Everything was-- you were dumped into a concentration camp if you said, boo, almost.
And everybody was very orderly.
You could walk on the streets in the middle of the night almost, you know?
But I arrived at this difficult place.
It took me, oh, a long time to get there-- transfer in Berlin, transfer underway, and finally got to Silesia.
And as I arrived, I telephoned from the station-- I think it was late in the evening-- and said I've just come.
Shall I take-- and he said, take a taxi, so much I could understand.
Take a taxi and come over to our house.
I knew his address-- 58 Wilhelm-Strasse, [NON-ENGLISH], yes.
I went over to his house, a very lovely house with three apartments.
Each storey was an apartment.
He was a very well-to-do man, as he told me.
It was very interesting.
And he was sitting at the table with his governess for the little girl.
She was a delightful person, 14 years old.
She looked like the then queen of England.
She was a pretty child, and very lively and very peppy.
She was what the Germans, the Nazis, called 'mischling.' That's a word, actually, for dogs, I think.
She was half Jewish and half German.
But apparently, he felt he was not Jewish, and Hitler should accept that-- because he had become a Lutheran in order to be permitted to marry his Catholic wife.
OK.
Well, you can-- unless you're baptized, you're a Catholic-- anyhow, he was-- he said, I am not Jewish.
I don't know what he meant.
Probably thought I was Jewish.
I don't know what it was.
But it didn't bother me.
I accepted it.
But he told me also about himself.
You could only speak in little short sentences, because I didn't have much English and he, probably, didn't have too much to communicate to this strange young American girl.
And he was glad I was there to help little Ani-- Annamarie learn English.
And he was often traveling, took trips to Berlin.
He had been forced by the Nazis to sell his factory.
And when he was at home, it was really very pathetic.
He would sit around the table-- as I say, when I arrived that night, they were sitting around the table.
And they informed me that he had just come from the concentration camp.
And I knew of that from school, where we had lost all our students.
They were there-- I think he was there three weeks.
And he came back three weeks later.
And that was the night, strangely, that he had arrived, a few hours earlier.
And they had a dear friend, a doctor, an MD that was a friend of the family.
And he was there with them.

It was a big courageous deal for him to associate with his former-- his friend, the Jewish man, because he was a marked man in that town, primarily because he was Jewish and also because he was supposed to be so rich.
And he said, I don't know what Hitler has against me, said this poor man.
I don't know what Hitler has against me.
I have always kept away from politics.
I am strictly apolitical.
And I like the things that Hitler does-- you know, the Autobahn.
And the only thing I don't like is his kraft durch freude.
That means strength through joy.
And with that, Hitler financed the building of many big ships.
And to get the money out of everybody's pockets, he said it was for the people.
He was for the people, of course, Hitler.
And they could go on these wonderful trips to Rome in these KdF ships-- KdF ships, Kraft durch Freude.

And he had his file arranged alphabetically-- Australia, Brazil, it went right through the list of the countries that he was corresponding with, the diplomats of these countries to see if there would be any way that in such a country, if he got there-- he thought he could get there without any problem.
He thought his only sacrifice was that he wouldn't be able to take more than 10 marks out of Germany.
And he did come to some terms with the Brazilian diplomats.
They thought that he could make an arrangement with them.
Hmm?
I talk so much, huh?
